[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: NewHealthController()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the PostgreSQL health check uses SkipOnErr: false, meaning any DB error causes the entire readiness check to fail, and by causing intermittent DB connection errors (via connection flooding), make the readiness check flap between healthy and unhealthy, causing the orchestrator to repeatedly restart the Rosetta pod and interrupt staking reward calculations in progress?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the health check calls /network/list and /network/status sequentially within a single check invocation, and by causing the /network/list call to succeed but the /network/status call to fail (via targeted connection interference), cause the health check to return an error that with SkipOnErr: false marks the service as not ready, triggering a pod restart during staking reward processing?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: NewHealthController()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the health check's PostgreSQL DSN is constructed at startup via rosettaConfig.Db.GetDsn() and stored in the postgres.Config struct, and if the hellofresh/health-go library's postgres check package logs the DSN on connection failure, trigger a DB connection failure via flooding to cause the DSN (including plaintext password) to be written to application logs accessible via a log aggregation endpoint?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the health check's context (ctx context.Context) passed to NetworkList and NetworkStatus has no deadline beyond the outer 10-second health check timeout, and by causing the internal Rosetta server to respond slowly (via concurrent request flooding), cause the health check to always time out, marking the service as not ready and preventing staking reward transactions from being processed?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: Routes()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the health controller's Routes() function returns routes that are registered in the same router as all other Rosetta API endpoints, and by sending a flood of GET /health/readiness requests, cause the router's request queue to fill up, dropping incoming staking reward construction requests (/construction/submit) and preventing reward claims from being submitted on-chain?
